sections.heading: Definition and Anatomy
sections.content: Vaginal cancer is a rare gynecologic malignancy originating in the vagina, defined as primary carcinoma arising in the vaginal canal. It accounts for less than 1% of all cancers affecting individuals assigned female at birth (AFAB) worldwide [1]. The vagina is a fibromuscular tubular tract extending from the vulva to the cervix, with an embryological origin derived from the Müllerian (paramesonephric) ducts and the urogenital sinus. The proximal two-thirds of the vagina have a Müllerian origin, while the distal one-third develops from the urogenital sinus.

sections.heading: Staging and Differential
sections.content: The anatomical boundaries for staging include the vaginal wall itself, with extension to the paravaginal tissues, pelvic sidewall, bladder mucosa, rectal mucosa, and beyond the true pelvis representing advanced disease. Only a minority of vaginal cancers originate primarily in the vagina; many are metastatic or extensions from other sites, such as the cervix, vulva, or rectum.

sections.heading: Histologic Classification
sections.content: The predominant histologic type is squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), which accounts for approximately 80% to 90% of cases and most commonly arises in the upper portion of the posterior vaginal wall [6]. The 2020 World Health Organization (WHO) classification recommends distinguishing between HPV-associated and HPV-independent SCC of the vagina [11]. HPV-associated tumors are typically non-keratinizing and located in the proximal or intermediate third of the vagina (Müllerian portion), whereas HPV-independent distal tumors, also known as introitus carcinoma, are often keratinizing [11].

sections.heading: Diagnostic Workup
sections.content: Diagnosis is confirmed by biopsy, which remains the gold standard. Comprehensive workup includes history and physical, pelvic examination, and imaging to determine the extent of disease and exclude synchronous primaries or metastasis from other sites [VAG-1].
